title: Wet-felted needle case
subtitle: A flat merino case, 10 x 14 cm closed, with a wool-felt interior page for holding hand-sewing and knitting needles
excerpt: Wet-felt two flat merino panels, trim to size, stitch or needle-felt them together along three sides, and fold to make a compact case that holds hand-sewing needles and small notions. Uses around 30 g of roving and felts in under an hour.
sourceNotes: Flat-panel wet felting is documented in Victorian needlework manuals as a method for producing protective needle books. Observation-led adaptation for modern merino roving and kitchen-table felting.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres aligned in a continuous rope, ready for spinning or felting.

A felting method that uses hot water, soap, and physical agitation to cause wool fibres to lock together and form a dense fabric. The scales on wool fibres open in heat and interlock when rubbed together, producing felt without weaving or knitting.

content.text: needle case is two flat panels of merino felt joined on three sides to make a small book that holds needles and notions. The outer panel is usually a single colour and the inner page is a contrasting colour so needles are easy to see. The case folds flat to fit in a project bag or sewing kit. Made from
laid in two separate flat pieces and felted independently, then trimmed and joined. No cutting into complex shapes is needed.

items.name: Merino roving, inner contrast colour, 10 g
Hot water and olive-oil soap or wool wash
Strong wool thread and blunt darning needle for stitching (optional)

content.text: Step 1: Lay and felt the outer panel

Mark a rectangle 22 x 32 cm on the rolling mat (the panel will shrink to approximately 14 x 20 cm, which folds to a 10 x 14 cm case). Lay wisps of outer-colour roving horizontally across the rectangle in two crossing layers. Wet thoroughly with hot soapy water, cover with the tea towel, and press flat. Rub for 5 minutes through the cloth, then rub directly on the wet surface for a further 5 minutes until the fibres knit together. Roll up in the mat and roll back and forth 50 times. Unroll, turn 90 degrees, and roll 50 times more. Repeat until the panel measures approximately 14 x 20 cm and feels firm. Shock rinse in cold water.

Lay the contrast colour roving in a rectangle slightly smaller than the outer panel, approximately 18 x 28 cm. Felt in the same way. The inner page should shrink to approximately 12 x 18 cm: slightly smaller than the outer panel so the edges of the case remain unobstructed.

Once both panels are dry, trim the outer panel to 14 x 20 cm and the inner page to 12 x 18 cm using a ruler and scissors. Centre the inner page on the outer panel. Join them along the left edge and two short edges by needle-felting along the seam using the 38-gauge felting needle, stabbing along the boundary until the two layers bond. Alternatively, oversew the edges by hand with wool thread through a blunt darning needle in a running stitch, spacing stitches 5 mm apart. Leave the right edge open as the needle insertion side.

Fold the joined case in half along the left edge so the inner page is inside. The finished case opens like a book: the inner page holds needles pushed through its surface; the outer panel protects them. To close the case securely, thread a length of wool through the outer panel and knot it to make a loop, then fasten with a button through the loop.

items.problem: The two panels have felted to very different sizes
items.cause: The roving layers were different thicknesses or the rolling was uneven
items.fix: Continue felting the smaller panel alone until it matches, or trim both panels to the same size once dry. Minor size differences are easy to correct at the trim stage.

items.problem: The panels feel flimsy and do not hold needles firmly
items.cause: Insufficient agitation; the felt did not reach full density
items.fix: Rewet the panel in hot soapy water and continue rolling for another 10 minutes. Felt panels for needle cases need to be dense enough that a needle does not push straight through.
